perturbation.original_text: 10.1 Term. This Agreement shall commence as of the Effective Date and shall continue in effect for one (I) year, unless earlier terminated as expressly provided in Sections 1.3. 10.1. or 10.2 of this Agreement (the *Initial Term"). This Agreement shall automatically renew beyond the Initial Term for successive one (I) year terms (each, a "Renewal Term"), unless a Party provides the other with written notice of termination at least one hundred eighty (180) days prior to the expiration of the Initial Term or the then-current Renewal Term.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.1 Term. This Agreement shall commence as of the Effective Date and shall continue in effect for one (I) year, unless earlier terminated as expressly provided in Sections 1.3. 10.1. or 10.2 of this Agreement (the *Initial Term"). This Agreement shall automatically renew beyond the Initial Term for successive one (I) year terms (each, a "Renewal Term"), unless a Party provides the other with written notice of termination at least thirty (30) days prior to the expiration of the Initial Term or the then-current Renewal Term.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from 180 days to 30 days could render automatic renewal clauses unenforceable under certain state laws designed to protect businesses from evergreen contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 17602
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires specific disclosures for automatic renewal contracts, including clear and conspicuous notice of the automatic renewal provision and a method for cancellation. The 30-day notice might not provide sufficient time for the licensee to act, particularly if the initial contract lacked the legally required disclosures about the automatic renewal. This may make the clause unenforceable under California law.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 9. Automatic Purchase Renewals [17600 - 17606] ( Article 9 added by Stats. 2009, Ch. 350, Sec. 1. ) 17602. (a) It is unlawful for any business that makes an automatic renewal offer or continuous service offer to a consumer in this state to do any of the following: (1) Fail to present the automatic renewal offer terms or continuous service offer terms in a clear and conspicuous manner before the subscription or purchasing agreement is fulfilled and in visual proximity, or, in the case of an offer conveyed by voice, in temporal proximity, to the request for consent to the offer. If the offer also includes a free gift or trial, the offer shall include a clear and conspicuous explanation of the price that will be charged after the trial ends or the manner in which the subscription or purchasing agreement pricing will change upon conclusion of the trial. (2) Charge the consumer’s credit or debit card, or the consumer’s account with a third party, for an
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the termination notice period from 180 days to 30 days, potentially conflicting with California law regarding automatic renewal offers. The Law Explanation suggests that a 30-day notice might be insufficient, especially if the initial contract lacked clear disclosures about automatic renewal. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 17602(a), states that it is unlawful for a business to make an automatic renewal offer without presenting the terms in a clear and conspicuous manner. While the snippet doesn't directly specify a minimum notice period, the reduced notice period combined with potentially inadequate initial disclosures, as described in the Law Explanation, could render the automatic renewal clause unenforceable under California law, creating a legal contradiction as defined by the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition.
